1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the structure-sheaf unit are present as
89	scheme-level objects. Rather than the geometric local-triviality route, the
90	group law is built on \(\otimes\)-\emph{invertibility}: an object is a line
91	object when it admits a tensor inverse, \(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong
92	\mathcal{O}_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), exactly Mathlib's
93	\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom. Under this predicate requirement (3)
94	becomes the three monoidal \emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) ---
95	associator, unitors and braiding --- each obtained cheaply: the unitors and
96	braiding as \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) of the corresponding
97	presheaf-level isomorphism, and the associator (needed only on the locally
98	trivial line bundles the law multiplies) as a three-step composite that absorbs
99	the two nested sheafifications by applying the sheafification functor to the unit
100	morphism whiskered by a locally trivial object --- inverted because that
101	whiskered unit lies in the sheafification localizer class, checked on a
102	trivialising cover --- with the presheaf-level associator transported in between,
103	while the inverse is carried by the predicate itself.
104	These
105	assemble the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units group is
106	the Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The geometric
107	compatibility isomorphism \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\)
108	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and any separate tensor-inverse lemma are
109	\emph{off} this critical path. The full symmetric monoidal category on
110	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is also \emph{not} required; see
111	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
112	
113	\section{Mathlib API survey}
114	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
115	
116	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
117	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
118	
119	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
120	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
121	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
122	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
123	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
124	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
125	sectionwise tensor product
126	\[
127	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
128	\]
129	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
130	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
131	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
132	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
133	
134	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
135	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
136	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
137	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
138	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
139	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
140	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
141	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
142	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
143	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
144	tensor-product operation
145	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
146	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
147	
148	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
149	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
150	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
151	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
152	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
153	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The data this operation must supply for the group law are the monoidal
154	\emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) --- the associator
155	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the left/right unitors
156	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) and the braiding
157	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The unitors and braiding are obtained by the
158	cheap pattern \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) applied to the corresponding
159	presheaf-level isomorphism on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\); the associator,
160	restricted to the \emph{locally trivial} line bundles the group law actually
161	multiplies, is obtained as the three-step composite of
162	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: apply the sheafification functor to the unit
163	morphism whiskered by a locally trivial object to absorb each of the two nested
164	sheafifications (once on each side), with the presheaf-level associator
165	transported by \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) in between. The only non-formal
166	input is that the whiskered sheafification unit lies in the localizer class
167	\(J.W\): local surjectivity is free (right-exactness of \(\otimes\)), and local
168	injectivity is local-on-target, so it is checked after passing to a trivialising
169	cover on which the whiskering object is the structure sheaf and the whiskered
170	unit is carried by the unitor onto the locally injective sheafification unit
171	itself; the closed bridge \cref{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W} then inverts it
172	under sheafification. This uses no flatness hypothesis. Neither route uses the
173	abstract \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) adjoint, a
174	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure on presheaves of modules, or a bundled
175	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) instance on the localizer class.
176	Together with the
177	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
178	(which carries the inverse existentially) these assemble the commutative monoid
179	of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units group
180	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The project does \emph{not} require a
181	global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance on all quasi-coherent
182	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure:
183	monoidal coherence beyond these three isomorphisms is never consumed by a group
184	law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The
185	open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
186	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is \emph{not} on this critical path and is
187	retained only as an optional supplement.
188	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
189	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
190	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
191	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
192	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
193	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
194	independently of any upstream timing.
195	
196	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
197	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
198	
199	\begin{definition}
200	
201	\leanok
202	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
203	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
205	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
206	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
207	  \[
208	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
209	  \]
210	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
211	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
212	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
213	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
214	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
215	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
216	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
217	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
218	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
219	  canonical compatibility
220	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
221	     \;\cong\;
222	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
223	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
224	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
225	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
226	
227	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
228	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
229	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
230	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
231	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
232	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
233	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
234	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
235	\end{definition}
236	
237	\begin{lemma}
238	
239	\leanok
240	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
241	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
242	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
243	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
244	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
245	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
246	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
247	  \[
248	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
249	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
250	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
251	  \]
252	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
253	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
254	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
255	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
256	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
257	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
258	  \[
259	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
260	    \qquad
261	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
262	  \]
263	  the associator
264	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
265	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
266	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
267	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
268	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
269	\end{lemma}
270	
271	\begin{proof}
272	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
273	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
274	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
275	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
276	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
277	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
278	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
279	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
280	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
281	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
282	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
283	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
284	\end{proof}
285	
286	\begin{remark}
287	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
288	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
289	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
290	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
291	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
292	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
293	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
294	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
295	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
296	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
297	  depend on it.
298	
299	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
300	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
301	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
302	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
303	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
304	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
305	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
306	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
307	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
308	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
309	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
310	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
311	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
312	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
313	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
314	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
315	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
316	
317	  Crucially, the group law restricts that stability to \emph{locally trivial}
318	  whiskering objects, where it becomes elementary and needs no closed structure:
319	  for a locally trivial \(F\) the implication \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft
320	  g)\), specialised to \(g = \eta\) a sheafification unit, holds because the
321	  whiskered unit lies in \(J.W\) --- local surjectivity is free
322	  (right-exactness of \(\otimes\)) and local injectivity is local-on-target,
323	  checked after passing to a cover on which \(F\) is the structure sheaf and the
324	  whiskered unit is carried by the unitor onto the locally injective unit itself
325	  (the argument of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). This locally-trivial-scoped
326	  stability is all the group law's associator
327	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}) consumes, and it sidesteps the
328	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) wall that the arbitrary-\(F\) version would hit, as
329	  well as the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
330	  (which is false over a non-affine open and is therefore off the associator
331	  critical path).
332	
333	  The group law needs only the coherence isomorphisms
334	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}:
335	  the unitors and braiding by \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) of the
336	  presheaf-level isomorphisms, and the associator by the locally-trivial-scoped
337	  three-step composite above. None of these is built from the off-path
338	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
339	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
340	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
341	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
342	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
343	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
344	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
345	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
346	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
347	\end{remark}
348	
349	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
350	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
351	
352	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
353	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
354	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
355	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
356	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
357	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the restriction-compatibility
358	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
359	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
360	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
361	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
362	cover, together with their restriction to the
363	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
364	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
365	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
366	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
367	
368	\begin{lemma}
369	
370	\leanok
371	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
372	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
373	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
374	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
375	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
376	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
377	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
378	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
379	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
380	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
381	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
382	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
383	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
384	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
385	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
386	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
387	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
388	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
389	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
390	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
391	  \[
392	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
393	      \;\;:=\;\;
394	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
395	  \]
396	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
397	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
398	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
399	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
400	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
401	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
402	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
403	\end{lemma}
404	
405	\begin{proof}
406	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
407	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
408	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
409	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
410	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
411	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
412	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
413	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
414	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
415	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
416	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
417	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
418	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
419	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
420	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
421	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
422	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
423	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
424	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
425	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
426	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
427	
428	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
429	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
430	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
431	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
432	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
433	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
434	\end{proof}
435	
436	\begin{lemma}
437	
438	\leanok
439	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
440	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
442	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
443	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
444	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
445	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
446	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
447	  morphism
448	  \[
449	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
450	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
451	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
452	  \]
453	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
454	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
455	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
456	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
457	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
458	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
459	\end{lemma}
460	
461	\begin{proof}
462	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
463	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
464	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
465	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
466	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
467	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
468	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
469	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
470	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
471	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
472	  isomorphism}
473	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
474	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
475	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
476	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
477	  therefore extension of scalars along
478	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
479	
480	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
481	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
482	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
483	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
484	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
485	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
486	  the abstract pullback functor.
487	
488	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
489	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
490	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
491	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
492	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
493	   \;\cong\;
494	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
495	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
496	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
497	  comparison
498	  \[
499	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
500	      \;\longrightarrow\;
501	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
502	      \;\otimes\;
503	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
504	  \]
505	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
506	
507	  \emph{Step 3 (off the critical path).} Completing this comparison at the
508	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise description of the abstract functor
509	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
510	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the
511	  pinned commit. The genuine residual decomposes into two Mathlib-absent
512	  presheaf-level ingredients --- a presheaf-level identification
513	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) via
514	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (needing a presheaf-level pushforward
515	  adjunction, only the sheaf version of which exists), and a strong-monoidal
516	  upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the ring isomorphism
517	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\). This lemma is therefore \emph{not} on the critical path
518	  for the relative Picard group law: the group law is built from
519	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) and the
520	  coherence isomorphisms
521	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
522	  none of which consume restriction-compatibility. The statement is retained
523	  only as an optional supplement; its full proof is deferred.
524	\end{proof}
525	
526	\begin{lemma}
527	
528	\leanok
529	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
530	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
531	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
532	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
533	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
534	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
535	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
536	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
537	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
538	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
539	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
540	\end{lemma}
541	
542	\begin{proof}
543	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
544	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
545	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
546	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
547	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
548	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
549	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
550	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
551	\end{proof}
552	
553	\begin{lemma}
554	
555	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
556	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
557	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_flat, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_flat}
558	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
559	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
560	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
561	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
562	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
563	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
564	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
565	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
566	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
567	  left-whiskered morphism
568	  \[
569	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
570	  \]
571	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
572	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
573	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
574	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
575	
576	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
577	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
578	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
579	  however, \emph{off the critical path} for the associator
580	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: that lemma is now proved on \emph{locally
581	  trivial} objects by a local-on-cover injectivity argument, which never
582	  invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of the present lemma. The
583	  sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over \(R(U)\) for \emph{every}
584	  object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens --- is not supplied by
585	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are affine-local
586	  properties; the present lemma is therefore retained as an independent result and
587	  not used in the associator construction.
588	\end{lemma}
589	
590	\begin{proof}
591	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
592	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
593	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
594	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
595	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
596	
597	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
598	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
599	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
600	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
601	  \[
602	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
603	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
604	  \]
605	
606	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
607	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
608	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
609	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
610	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
611	
612	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
613	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
614	  linear maps (Mathlib
615	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
616	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
617	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
618	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
619	
620	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
621	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
622	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
623	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
624	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
625	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
626	\end{proof}
627	
628	\begin{lemma}
629	
630	\leanok
631	[Sheafification inverts a localizer morphism of presheaves of modules]
632	  \label{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}
633	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isIso_sheafification_map_of_W}
634	  Let \(R_0\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a
635	  Grothendieck topology \(J\), let \(R_{sh}\) be its sheafification, and let
636	  \(\alpha : R_0 \to R_{sh}.\mathrm{obj}\) be the (locally bijective) comparison
637	  map of presheaves of rings. Let \(f : A \to B\) be a morphism of presheaves of
638	  \(R_0\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
639	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\) lies in the sheafification
640	  localizer class \(J.W\) --- equivalently, is locally bijective for \(J\). Then
641	  the presheaf-of-modules sheafification functor
642	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\,\alpha\) sends \(f\) to an
643	  isomorphism:
644	  \[
645	    (\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f
646	    \quad\text{is an isomorphism.}
647	  \]
648	  This is the go/no-go bridge consumed by the two absorption steps of the
649	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: it converts ``\,whiskered unit lies in
650	  \(J.W\)\,'' into ``\,its sheafification is invertible\,''.
651	\end{lemma}
652	
653	\begin{proof}
654	  This is a one-morphism reading of the structural identity that the
655	  sheafification of presheaves of \(R_0\)-modules \emph{is} the localization of
656	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R_0\) at the class
657	  \(J.W.\mathtt{inverseImage}\,(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0)\) of morphisms whose
658	  underlying map of presheaves of abelian groups is locally bijective. Concretely,
659	  the underlying \(\mathtt{AddCommGrp}\)-valued sheafification inverts exactly the
660	  locally bijective morphisms, and Mathlib's
661	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.inverseImage\_W\_toPresheaf\_eq\_inverseImage\_%
662	  isomorphisms}\) identifies the preimage of \(J.W\) under
663	  \(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0\) with the preimage of the class of isomorphisms under
664	  the module-level sheafification functor. Reading that equality of morphism
665	  classes at the single morphism \(f\): the hypothesis
666	  \(J.W\,\bigl((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\bigr)\) places \(f\) in
667	  that preimage, hence \((\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f\) is an
668	  isomorphism. No new construction is required beyond instantiating the existing
669	  Mathlib identity at one morphism; this lemma is the thin wrapper used by the
670	  absorption steps of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}.
671	\end{proof}

676	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on locally trivial objects]
677	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
678	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
679	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:IsLocallyTrivial,
680	        lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}
681	  % NOTE (iter-213): the Lean body now implements ROUTE (d) (flatness-free,
682	  % stalkwise), NOT the locally-trivial-cover route this proof block describes.
683	  % The whiskered-unit localizer step holds for an ARBITRARY module via the new
684	  % lemmas W_whiskerLeft_of_W / W_whiskerRight_of_W (closed), so the
685	  % IsLocallyTrivial hypotheses on M,N,P are received but UNUSED (retained only to
686	  % match this pin). The sole remaining open obligation is the unpinned residual
687	  % PresheafOfModules.isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W (a `sorry`: stalkwise
688	  % id ⊗ g_x iso; needs Mathlib-absent d.1 module-level J.W↔stalk-iso bridge and
689	  % d.2 stalk-commutes-with-relative-tensor). Blueprint-writer should rewrite this
690	  % proof to ROUTE (d) and add lemma blocks for the three WhiskerOfW decls. See
691	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-ts213.md.
692	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
693	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{locally trivial}
694	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}): each
695	  admits an open cover on which it restricts to the structure sheaf. There is an
696	  isomorphism
697	  \[
698	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
699	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
700	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
701	  \]
702	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). This is an \emph{objectwise}
703	  existence-of-isomorphism statement for the three fixed locally trivial objects
704	  \(M, N, P\) --- not a natural associator on a category --- and that is
705	  exactly the datum consumed by the group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
706	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), where associativity is the
707	  proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong
708	  M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\). No pentagon identity is asserted.
709	\end{lemma}
710	
711	\begin{proof}
712	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:IsLocallyTrivial,
713	        lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}
714	  The associator is built directly from the presheaf-of-modules monoidal data by
715	  absorbing the two nested sheafifications. The only non-formal input is that the
716	  whiskered sheafification unit lies in the sheafification localizer class
717	  \(J.W\); this is proved at the presheaf/section level on a trivialising cover,
718	  using local triviality of the whiskering object and no flatness hypothesis.
719	
720	  \emph{Notation.} Write \(a = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) for the
721	  sheafification functor and \(M^{\flat}\) for the underlying presheaf of an
722	  object \(M\), so that the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is
723	  \(M \otimes_X N = a\bigl(M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}\bigr)\), with
724	  \(\otimes^{p}\) the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
725	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}). Let
726	  \(\eta = \mathtt{toSheafify}\) be the sheafification unit, the canonical
727	  morphism \(A \to a(A)^{\flat}\); it lies in the sheafification localizer class
728	  \(J.W\), being locally injective (\(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_toSheafify}\))
729	  and locally surjective. Let \(\alpha\) be the associator of the
730	  presheaf-of-modules monoidal category,
731	  \[
732	    \alpha \;\colon\;
733	      (M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}) \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}
734	        \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
735	      M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} (N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}).
736	  \]
737	
738	  \emph{The key claim: a whiskered unit stays in \(J.W\).} We show that for a
739	  \emph{locally trivial} object \(F\) and any presheaf \(A\) the right-whiskered
740	  unit \(\eta_A \triangleright F^{\flat}\) lies in \(J.W\) (and symmetrically the
741	  left-whiskered unit \(F^{\flat} \triangleleft \eta_A\)). By the locally-bijective
742	  characterisation of \(J.W\) it suffices to check local surjectivity and local
743	  injectivity.
744	
745	  \emph{Local surjectivity is free.} The presheaf tensor is right exact, so
746	  whiskering carries the (locally zero) cokernel of \(\eta_A\) to the cokernel of
747	  \(\eta_A \triangleright F^{\flat}\); hence the whiskered map is locally
748	  surjective (\(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)), with no hypothesis on
749	  \(F\).
750	
751	  \emph{Local injectivity is local on the target, and is supplied by the
752	  trivialising cover.} Local injectivity asks that the equalizer sieve of the
753	  morphism be a covering sieve; this is a \emph{local-on-target} condition, so it
754	  may be checked after passing to a cover. Since \(F\) is locally trivial
755	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}), there is an open cover \(\{V \hookrightarrow U\}\)
756	  of each open \(U\) on which \(F\) restricts to the structure sheaf,
757	  \(F^{\flat}|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_X|_V\) --- a sectionwise consequence of the
758	  trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_X|_V\) furnished by
759	  \(\mathtt{restrictIsoUnitOfLE}\). The presheaf tensor product is
760	  sectionwise/definitional,
761	  \[
762	    (A \otimes^{p} F^{\flat}).\mathrm{obj}\,V
763	      \;=\; A.\mathrm{obj}\,V \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(V)} F^{\flat}(V),
764	  \]
765	  so over \(V\) the trivialisation \(F^{\flat}(V) \cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) and the
766	  right unitor \(\rho\) of the presheaf monoidal structure carry
767	  \((\eta_A \triangleright F^{\flat})|_V\) onto \(\eta_A|_V\) itself. Now
768	  \(\eta_A = \mathtt{toSheafify}\) is locally injective
769	  (\(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_toSheafify}\)), hence so is its restriction to the
770	  cover; gluing the per-\(V\) injectivity along the cover, \(\eta_A \triangleright
771	  F^{\flat}\) is locally injective. (The sieve bookkeeping is exactly that of the
772	  flat-whiskering injectivity argument
773	  \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_flat}\); its
774	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_exact}\) injectivity step is the only thing
775	  swapped --- replaced here by the trivialisation
776	  \(F^{\flat}(V) \cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) on the cover and the right unitor.) The
777	  left-whiskered statement \(F^{\flat} \triangleleft \eta_A\) is identical with the
778	  left unitor \(\lambda\) in place of \(\rho\).
779	
780	  Local injectivity together with local surjectivity gives
781	  \(\eta_A \triangleright F^{\flat} \in J.W\) (resp.\ \(F^{\flat} \triangleleft
782	  \eta_A \in J.W\)). Applying the closed bridge
783	  \cref{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}, the sheafification \(a\) sends each such
784	  whiskered unit to an isomorphism.
785	
786	  \emph{The nested form.} Unfolding the substrate on both sides exposes an inner
787	  sheafification:
788	  \[
789	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
790	      \;=\; a\Bigl(\, a\bigl(M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}\bigr)^{\flat}
791	              \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\Bigr),
792	    \qquad
793	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
794	      \;=\; a\Bigl(\, M^{\flat} \otimes^{p}
795	              a\bigl(N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}\bigr)^{\flat} \,\Bigr).
796	  \]
797	  The required isomorphism is the three-step composite:
798	  \begin{enumerate}
799	    \item \emph{Absorb the inner sheafification on the left.} The unit
800	      \(\eta_{M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}}\) lies in \(J.W\); its
801	      right-whiskering by the locally trivial object \(P\),
802	      \(\eta_{M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}} \triangleright P^{\flat}\), again
803	      lies in \(J.W\) by the key claim above (\(P\) locally trivial). By
804	      \cref{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W} the sheafification functor \(a\)
805	      inverts it:
806	      \[
807	        a\Bigl(\, a\bigl(M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}\bigr)^{\flat}
808	              \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\Bigr)
809	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
810	        a\bigl(\, (M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}) \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\bigr).
811	      \]
812	    \item \emph{Transport the presheaf associator.} Apply \(a\) to \(\alpha\):
813	      \[
814	        a\bigl(\, (M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}) \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\bigr)
815	          \;\xrightarrow[\ \sim\ ]{\;a.\mathtt{mapIso}\,\alpha\;}\;
816	        a\bigl(\, M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} (N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}) \,\bigr).
817	      \]
818	    \item \emph{Restore the inner sheafification on the right.} The unit
819	      \(\eta_{N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}}\) lies in \(J.W\); its
820	      left-whiskering by the locally trivial object \(M\),
821	      \(M^{\flat} \triangleleft \eta_{N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}}\), again
Let me read the rest of the chapter.

822	      lies in \(J.W\) by the key claim above (\(M\) locally trivial). By
823	      \cref{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W} \(a\) inverts it:
824	      \[
825	        a\bigl(\, M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} (N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}) \,\bigr)
826	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
827	        a\Bigl(\, M^{\flat} \otimes^{p}
828	              a\bigl(N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}\bigr)^{\flat} \,\Bigr)
829	          \;=\; M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P).
830	      \]
831	  \end{enumerate}
832	  Composing the three isomorphisms yields
833	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim} M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\).
834	  Only steps 1 and 3 carry non-formal content, namely the key claim that the
835	  whiskered unit lies in \(J.W\); step 2 is the image under \(a\) of the presheaf
836	  monoidal associator already supplied by Mathlib.
837	
838	  This is an objectwise isomorphism for the three fixed locally trivial objects,
839	  not a natural associator on a category: no pentagon identity is constructed or
840	  used. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
841	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only the existence of this
842	  isomorphism, \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\), so this is all that is
843	  required. The construction works \emph{entirely at the presheaf/section level on
844	  a trivialising cover}: each absorption isomorphism comes from checking the
845	  local-on-target injectivity of a whiskered sheafification unit after restricting
846	  to a cover on which the whiskering object is the structure sheaf, then inverting
847	  under \(a\) via \cref{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}. It uses
848	  \emph{no} \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) and
849	  \emph{no} open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
850	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}): because the presheaf tensor is sectionwise,
851	  restriction to the cover is plain evaluation at smaller opens, and no
852	  sheafified-tensor pullback-compatibility iso is needed.
853	\end{proof}

1068	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1069	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1070	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1071	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1072	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1073	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1074	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1075	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1076	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1077	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1078	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1079	  same cover.
1080	\end{proof}
1081	
1082	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
1083	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
1084	
1085	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
1086	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
1087	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1088	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
1089	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
1090	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
1091	design template. The inverse of an invertible object is carried by the predicate
1092	itself, so neither the open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
1093	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a separate tensor-inverse lemma is on the
1094	critical path.
1095	
1096	\begin{definition}\leanok
1097	[\(\otimes\)-invertibility of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
1098	  \label{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
1099	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible}
1100	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1101	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
1102	  % modules), Definition 01CS (definition-invertible) + Lemma 0B8K
1103	  % (lemma-invertible, the "$\exists\,\mathcal{N}$" characterisation)
1104	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4079).
1105	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1106	  % "\begin{definition}

1111	  % the functor

1115	  % $$

1120	  % [\dots]

1136	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CS and Lemma~0B8K. An invertible
1137	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is one for which \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1138	  (-)\) is an equivalence; equivalently there exists \(\mathcal{N}\) with
1139	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and
1140	  then \(\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
1141	  \mathcal{O}_X)\).}
1142	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
1143	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
1144	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
1145	  \[
1146	    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
1147	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1148	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
1149	  \]
1150	  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
1151	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
1152	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
1153	  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
1154	  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
1155	  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
1156	  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
1157	  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
1158	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
1159	  \(\otimes_X\).

1162	\begin{lemma}[The commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units]
1163	  \label{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}
1164	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}
1165	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1166	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
1167	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
1168	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
1169	  % modules), Definition 01CX (definition-pic) + Lemma lemma-constructions-
1170	  % invertible (tensor of invertibles is invertible; dual is the inverse)
1171	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200-L4213 and L4350-L4357).
1172	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1173	  % "\begin{lemma}
1174	  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
1175	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1176	  % \begin{enumerate}
1177	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$, $\mathcal{N}$ are invertible
1178	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, then so is
1179	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N}$.
1180	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
1181	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
1182	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1183	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
1184	  % is an isomorphism.
1185	  % \end{enumerate}
1186	  % \end{lemma}
1187	  % [\dots]
1188	  % \begin{definition}
1189	  % \label{definition-pic}
1190	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1191	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
1192	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
1193	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
1194	  % corresponding to tensor product.
1195	  % \end{definition}"
1196	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1197	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1198	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1199	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit
1200	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and inverse the dual; the tensor product of invertibles is
1201	  invertible and the dual is a tensor inverse.}
1202	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1203	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1204	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1205	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1206	  equivalently, an abelian group. Multiplication is
1207	  \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\), well-defined on isomorphism classes
1208	  because \(\otimes_X\) is a bifunctor
1209	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, which transports
1210	  \(M \cong M'\) to \(M \otimes_X N \cong M' \otimes_X N\) and symmetrically)
1211	  and the tensor product of two \(\otimes\)-invertibles is again
1212	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (closure); the unit is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and
1213	  associativity, unitality and commutativity are the coherence isomorphisms
1214	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (on the invertible objects in play),
1215	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, read as
1216	  equalities of iso-classes. The inverse of \([M]\) is the class of the tensor
1217	  inverse \(N\) with \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) carried existentially
1218	  by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), so
1219	  every element is a unit. This abelian group is the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\)
1220	  (Stacks tag 01CR). It is, equivalently, the group of units of the commutative
1221	  monoid of \emph{all} iso-classes; that global monoid is not built here, since
1222	  the associator on arbitrary modules is not constructed and the group law
1223	  consumes only the objectwise coherence isomorphisms on invertible objects.
1224	  This mirrors Mathlib's
1225	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\)
1226	  and the transported \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\), used here as the design
1227	  template; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at
1228	  the pinned commit.
1229	
1230	  The associativity datum this group law consumes is the locally-trivial-scoped
1231	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}. If the \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
1232	  carrier form is retained for the group law, the only added obligation is the
1233	  bridge \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) --- the
1234	  standard fact that an invertible sheaf is locally free of rank one --- which
1235	  supplies the local-triviality hypotheses of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; this
1236	  bridge is itself off the associator critical path.
1237	\end{lemma}
1238	
1239	\begin{proof}
1240	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1241	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
1242	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
1243	  Restrict attention to \(\otimes\)-invertible objects. Closure holds: if
1244	  \(M, N\) are \(\otimes\)-invertible with inverses \(M', N'\), then
1245	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X (M' \otimes_X N') \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) by the
1246	  coherence isomorphisms, so \(M \otimes_X N\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible.
1247	  Well-definedness of \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\) on iso-classes is the
1248	  bifunctoriality of \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}. The
1249	  group axioms are propositions about iso-classes and are discharged by the
1250	  coherence isomorphisms, each evaluated on the invertible objects in play:
1251	  associativity by \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (whose hypotheses are exactly
1252	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility of the three arguments), the unit laws by
1253	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity by
1254	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}; no pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
1255	  invoked, since each axiom asserts only the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism.
1256	  Every class \([M]\) has an inverse \([N]\) with
1257	  \([M \otimes_X N] = [\mathcal{O}_X]\), namely the tensor inverse carried by
1258	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}).
1259	  Therefore the iso-classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible modules form an abelian
1260	  group --- the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- equivalently the
1261	  group of units of the (un-built) commutative monoid of all iso-classes. The
1262	  whole assembly follows the Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\)
1263	  template.
1264	\end{proof}

1266	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
1267	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
1268	
1269	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
1270	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
1271	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
1272	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1273	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
1274	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
1275	
1276	\begin{theorem}
1277	
1278	\leanok
1279	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
1280	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
1281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
1282	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1283	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1284	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1285	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1286	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
1287	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
1288	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
1289	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1290	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1291	
1292	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
1293	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
1294	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
1295	  % groups)
1296	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
1297	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1298	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
1299	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
1300	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
1301	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
1302	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
1303	  % \end{dfn}
1304	  %  [\dots]
1305	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
1306	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
1307	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
1308	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
1309	  % topologies  by
1310	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
1311	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
1312	  %  \end{dfn}"
1313	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
1314	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
1315	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
1316	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
1317	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
1318	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
1319	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
1320	  \[
1321	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
1322	  \]
1323	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
1324	  abelian-group structure with addition
1325	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1326	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
1327	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
1328	  inverse
1329	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
1330	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
1331	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1332	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
1333	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
1334	  map
1335	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
1336	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
1337	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1338	\end{theorem}
1339	
1340	\begin{proof}
1341	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1342	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1343	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1344	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1345	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1346	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1347	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
1348	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
1349	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
1350	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
1351	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
1352	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
1353	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
1354	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
1355	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
1356	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
1357	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
1358	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
1359	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
1360	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1361	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
1362	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
1363	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
1364	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
1365	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
1366	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
1367	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
1368	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
1369	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
1370	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
1371	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
1372	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
1373	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
1374	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
1375	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
1376	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
1377	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
1378	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
1379	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
1380	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
1381	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
1382	
1383	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
1384	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
1385	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
1386	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
1387	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
1388	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
1389	
1390	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
1391	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
1392	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
1393	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
1394	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
1395	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
1396	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
1397	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
1398	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
1399	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1400	\end{proof}

1435	\section{Internal-consistency check}
1436	\label{sec:tensorobj_consistency_check}
1437	
1438	The Lean-pinned declaration blocks of this chapter form a connected
1439	\texttt{\textbackslash uses\{\dots\}} chain whose leaves all resolve
1440	either inside this chapter, inside chapter

926	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
927	  % "\begin{lemma}
928	  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}

930	  % ...
931	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is

940	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the

945	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR, Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
946	  invertible} (item~2, the dual of an invertible is invertible and the contraction
947	  is an isomorphism) and Definition~\texttt{definition-pic} (the Picard group is
948	  the abelian group of iso-classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules under

950	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
951	  bundle. The dual
952	  \[
953	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
954	  \]
955	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
956	  isomorphism
957	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
958	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
959	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
960	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
961	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).

964	\begin{proof}
965	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
966	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
967	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
968	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
969	  The contraction map
970	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
971	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
972	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
973	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
974	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
975	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
976	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
977	\end{proof}

996	  % NOTE: the coherence isos
997	  % \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
998	  % and the units-of-monoid engine \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} supply
999	  % the group-law data. \texttt{IsLocallyTrivial} is a separate predicate,

1001	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes. The carrier is the
1002	  \(\otimes\)-invertible subtype
1003	  \[
1004	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1005	    \;\;=\;\;

1007	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\}
1008	  \]

1010	  \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of

1012	  if \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\) and \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M'\) then
1013	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M \otimes_{C \times_S T} M')\); the unit
1014	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\) is invertible; and every invertible module

1020	\begin{proof}
1021	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1022	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1023	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1024	  Closure under \(\otimes\) is the units-closed-under-multiplication statement of
1025	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}: if \(M \otimes N \cong \mathcal{O}\)
1026	  and \(M' \otimes N' \cong \mathcal{O}\), then
1027	  \((M \otimes M') \otimes (N \otimes N') \cong \mathcal{O}\) by the coherence
1028	  isomorphisms, so \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M \otimes M')\) with witness
1029	  \(N \otimes N'\). The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\) is invertible with
1030	  witness itself (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the tensor inverse is
1031	  carried by the predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). Thus the
1032	  subtype is a submonoid all of whose elements are units --- the abelian group
1033	  of iso-classes that is the units group of
1034	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} restricted to \(C \times_S T\).

1037	\begin{lemma}
1038	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1039	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1040	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1041	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1042	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1043	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1044	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1045	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1046	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1047	  functor
1048	  \[
1049	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1050	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1051	  \]
1052	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1053	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1054	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1055	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1056	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1057	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1058	  canonical isomorphism
1059	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1060	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1061	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1062	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
1063	\end{lemma}
1064	
1065	\begin{proof}
1066	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1067	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1068	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1069	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1070	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1071	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1072	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1073	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1074	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1075	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1076	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1077	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1078	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1079	  same cover.
1080	\end{proof}

1082	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
1083	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
1084	
1085	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
1086	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
1087	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1088	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
1089	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
1090	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
1091	design template. The inverse of an invertible object is carried by the predicate
1092	itself, so neither the open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
1093	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a separate tensor-inverse lemma is on the
1094	critical path.

1100	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

1114	  % \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
1115	  % $$

1136	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CS and Lemma~0B8K. An invertible
1137	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is one for which \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1138	  (-)\) is an equivalence; equivalently there exists \(\mathcal{N}\) with
1139	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and

1142	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
1143	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
1144	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
1145	  \[

1148	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
1149	  \]

1151	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong

1154	  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;

1156	  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
1157	  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
1158	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
1159	  \(\otimes_X\).
